label: Minigames
title: Minigame API
lead: Shared helpers for creating custom minigames on top of the game's vanilla Minigame base.
bodyHtml:
What this wraps

CUCoreLib does not replace the game's minigame system. It now mirrors the game's real split more closely: the vanilla Minigame contract still does the actual work, while CUCoreLib adds a runner facade, a live session object, and a definition layer so mods can target stable seams instead of poking globals directly.

The important vanilla hooks are Start(), Update(), PhysicsUpdate(), HandType(), GuideLocaleString(), NeedsItem(), and CanExit().

For quick visual wiring, the session layer exposes the live hand sprite, hand state, spawned minigame root, and the shared screen factory so custom minigames can swap art or grab child GameObjects without repeating reflection code everywhere.

CUCoreLib helper surface

HelperWhat it does
CUCoreMinigamesStatic runner facade that starts or ends minigames and exposes the current live session.
CUCoreMinigameSessionLive wrapper around the shared runner with access to body, current item, hand state, spawned UI, and screen/hand helpers.
CUCoreMinigameDefinitionComposable definition layer for new minigames. Override only the hooks you need and work against the session object.
CUCoreMinigames.TryStart(...) / TryStartDefinition(...)Starts either a vanilla Minigame or a definition-based CUCoreLib minigame only when the shared runner is idle.
session.TryCreateScreen(...)Loads a minigame screen prefab through the game's existing UI system.
session.End()Ends the active minigame through the shared runner.
session.TrySetHandSprite(...)Swaps the current hand sprite immediately, either from a sprite asset or one of the game's existing hand slots.
session.TryGetSpawnedMiniGameObject(...)Gets a child GameObject from the spawned minigame root by index.
CUCoreMinigameLegacy-compatible abstract wrapper for mods that still prefer direct inheritance from a Minigame-style base.

Minimal custom minigame

For new work, subclass CUCoreMinigameDefinition and start it through CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(...). That keeps your minigame logic focused on one live session object instead of scattered global calls.

using System.Collections.Generic;
using CUCoreLib.Helpers;
using UnityEngine;
using UnityEngine.EventSystems;

public sealed class WireSpliceMinigame : CUCoreMinigameDefinition
{
    public override Minigame.HandSpriteType HandType(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        return Minigame.HandSpriteType.Tweezers;
    }

    public override bool NeedsItem(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        return false;
    }

    public override string GuideLocaleKey(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        return "wireSpliceGuide";
    }

    public override void Start(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        session.TryCreateScreen("Special/WireSpliceMinigame");
    }

    public override void Update(CUCoreMinigameSession session, List<RaycastResult> uiCasts)
    {
        if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Escape) && CanExit(session))
        {
            session.End();
        }
    }
}

Starting a minigame

Use the runner guard so you do not accidentally stack minigames. If the runner is busy, the start request fails cleanly.

if (CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(() => new WireSpliceMinigame()))
{
    // Started successfully.
}

Compatibility path

If you already have a custom class that wants to inherit directly from Minigame-style helpers, CUCoreMinigame still works and now delegates through the same runner/session layer. Use it when direct subclassing is the least awkward fit, but prefer the definition/session surface for new APIs.

Built-in overlap

CUCoreLib already maps BandageProperties to BandageMinigame and SyringeProperties to SyringeMinigame. Those are the best examples to copy if your custom minigame needs a custom hand type, item requirement, or screen prefab.

For building interactions, the vanilla Openable flow still handles keypad and lockpick minigames. The helper framework is meant to make new custom minigames easier, not replace that existing behavior.

It now mirrors the game's real split more closely: the vanilla Minigame contract still does the actual work, while CUCoreLib adds a runner facade, a live session object, and a definition layer so mods can target stable seams instead of poking globals directly. The important vanilla hooks are Start() , Update() , PhysicsUpdate() , HandType() , GuideLocaleString() , NeedsItem() , and CanExit() . For quick visual wiring, the session layer exposes the live hand sprite, hand state, spawned minigame root, and the shared screen factory so custom minigames can swap art or grab child GameObjects without repeating reflection code everywhere. CUCoreLib helper surface Helper What it does CUCoreMinigames Static runner facade that starts or ends minigames and exposes the current live session. CUCoreMinigameSession Live wrapper around the shared runner with access to body, current item, hand state, spawned UI, and screen/hand helpers. CUCoreMinigameDefinition Composable definition layer for new minigames. Override only the hooks you need and work against the session object. CUCoreMinigames.TryStart(...) / TryStartDefinition(...) Starts either a vanilla Minigame or a definition-based CUCoreLib minigame only when the shared runner is idle. session.TryCreateScreen(...) Loads a minigame screen prefab through the game's existing UI system. session.End() Ends the active minigame through the shared runner. session.TrySetHandSprite(...) Swaps the current hand sprite immediately, either from a sprite asset or one of the game's existing hand slots. session.TryGetSpawnedMiniGameObject(...) Gets a child GameObject from the spawned minigame root by index. CUCoreMinigame Legacy-compatible abstract wrapper for mods that still prefer direct inheritance from a Minigame -style base. Minimal custom minigame For new work, subclass CUCoreMinigameDefinition and start it through CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(...) . That keeps your minigame logic focused on one live session object instead of scattered global calls. using System.Collections.Generic; using CUCoreLib.Helpers; using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.EventSystems; public sealed class WireSpliceMinigame : CUCoreMinigameDefinition { public override Minigame.HandSpriteType HandType(CUCoreMinigameSession session) { return Minigame.HandSpriteType.Tweezers; } public override bool NeedsItem(CUCoreMinigameSession session) { return false; } public override string GuideLocaleKey(CUCoreMinigameSession session) { return "wireSpliceGuide"; } public override void Start(CUCoreMinigameSession session) { session.TryCreateScreen("Special/WireSpliceMinigame"); } public override void Update(CUCoreMinigameSession session, List<RaycastResult> uiCasts) { if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Escape) && CanExit(session)) { session.End(); } } } Starting a minigame Use the runner guard so you do not accidentally stack minigames. If the runner is busy, the start request fails cleanly. if (CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(() => new WireSpliceMinigame())) { // Started successfully. } Compatibility path If you already have a custom class that wants to inherit directly from Minigame -style helpers, CUCoreMinigame still works and now delegates through the same runner/session layer. Use it when direct subclassing is the least awkward fit, but prefer the definition/session surface for new APIs. Built-in overlap CUCoreLib already maps BandageProperties to BandageMinigame and SyringeProperties to SyringeMinigame . Those are the best examples to copy if your custom minigame needs a custom hand type, item requirement, or screen prefab. For building interactions, the vanilla Openable flow still handles keypad and lockpick minigames. The helper framework is meant to make new custom minigames easier, not replace that existing behavior.
